sections.heading: Violent discipline fell nationwide, while non-violent discipline increased
sections.body: In 2025, 54.7 percent of children aged 1 to 14 years experienced some form of violent discipline, down from 58.8 percent in 2022. Each violent component also declined: psychological aggression fell from 48.4 to 43.6 percent, any physical punishment from 39.1 to 36.1 percent, and severe physical punishment from 3.4 to 2.0 percent. Only non-violent discipline increased, rising from 35.7 to 39.0 percent. Overall, the country improved, but violent discipline still affected more than half of children.

sections.heading: Regional levels ranged from about one-third to more than seven in ten children
sections.body: In 2025, any violent discipline varied widely by region. It was 32.7 percent in BARMM and 34.9 percent in Central Visayas, but reached 70.9 percent in Davao Region, 66.8 percent in Cagayan Valley, and 66.4 percent in both Bicol Region and Caraga. These differences were not limited to one type of discipline. Davao Region had the highest psychological aggression level at 60.3 percent and physical punishment at 44.7 percent, while BARMM was lower on both psychological aggression, at 27.7 percent, and physical punishment, at 19.9 percent. The gap between regions was much larger than the national change over time.

sections.heading: Some regions saw sharp declines from 2022 to 2025
sections.body: The biggest drops in any violent discipline were in Zamboanga Peninsula¹, Central Visayas, and Ilocos Region. Zamboanga Peninsula fell from 72.3 percent in 2022 to 41.9 percent in 2025, while psychological aggression dropped from 60.0 to 34.7 percent and any physical punishment dropped from 58.1 to 27.3 percent. Central Visayas fell from 59.9 to 34.9 percent, while only non-violent discipline rose, from 39.3 to 59.4 percent. Ilocos Region also declined, from 66.3 to 52.3 percent. These patterns show that regional change was uneven, but it could be large when both psychological and physical components went down.

sections.heading: Some regions went up despite the national decline
sections.body: Caraga moved in the opposite direction from the national trend. Any violent discipline rose from 59.7 percent in 2022 to 66.4 percent in 2025, with psychological aggression rising from 50.8 to 58.4 percent and any physical punishment rising from 32.0 to 38.9 percent. BARMM also increased, from 28.3 to 32.7 percent, even though it remained the lowest region in 2025. Its increase came from psychological aggression rising from 23.3 to 27.7 percent, while physical punishment fell from 21.6 to 19.9 percent. Davao Region stayed very high, moving from 69.2 to 70.9 percent, with psychological aggression increasing and severe physical punishment declining. These mixed movements show why the headline rate should be read together with the specific discipline methods behind it.

sections.heading: Severe physical punishment was uncommon, but still present
sections.body: Severe physical punishment was much less common than psychological aggression or any physical punishment in every region, but it was still reported across the map. In 2025, the highest values were 3.3 percent in Negros Island Region, 2.8 percent each in Eastern Visayas and Northern Mindanao, and 2.6 percent each in Central Luzon and Caraga. The lowest regional values were 1.0 percent in Ilocos Region and 1.2 percent in Davao Region. This means regional differences in violent discipline were driven mostly by psychological aggression and non-severe physical punishment, while severe physical punishment remained a smaller but continuing part of the pattern.

sections.heading: Children aged 3 to 9 were exposed the most
sections.body: Age mattered in 2025. Any violent discipline was 42.3 percent among children aged 1 to 2 years, then rose to 59.2 percent among children aged 3 to 4 and 58.5 percent among children aged 5 to 9, before easing to 53.1 percent among children aged 10 to 14. The components help explain this pattern: psychological aggression was much lower for ages 1 to 2, at 25.5 percent, than for older groups, while physical punishment was highest at 46.2 percent among ages 3 to 4 and 42.2 percent among ages 5 to 9. All four age groups had lower violent discipline in 2025 than in 2022.

sections.heading: Boys experienced more violent discipline than girls
sections.body: In 2025, male children had higher exposure to any violent discipline, at 57.1 percent, compared with 52.0 percent among female children. The gap showed up in both major components: psychological aggression was 45.4 percent for boys and 41.5 percent for girls, while any physical punishment was 38.9 percent for boys and 32.9 percent for girls. Severe physical punishment was also slightly higher for boys, at 2.3 percent, compared with 1.8 percent for girls. Both sexes improved from 2022, but the gap between boys and girls remained.

sections.heading: Urban children were slightly higher than rural children
sections.body: In 2025, any violent discipline was 56.2 percent among urban children, compared with 53.1 percent among rural children. Psychological aggression was almost the same in urban and rural areas, at 43.8 and 43.4 percent. Physical punishment was higher in urban areas, at 39.1 percent compared with 32.9 percent in rural areas. Only non-violent discipline was higher in rural areas, at 40.8 percent compared with 37.4 percent in urban areas. Both residence groups had lower violent discipline than in 2022, so the urban-rural difference was smaller than the regional divide.

sections.heading: Mother's education did not show a simple pattern
sections.body: In 2025, any violent discipline did not simply fall as mothers' schooling increased. It ranged from 46.1 percent among children whose mothers had no education to 58.8 percent among children whose mothers had post-secondary education and 58.1 percent among children whose mothers had Grades 7 to 10. Children whose mothers had college education were at 53.5 percent, while children whose mothers were not in the household were at 49.4 percent. The components also varied: post-secondary had the highest psychological aggression level at 50.9 percent, while Grades 11 to 12 and post-secondary had the highest physical punishment levels at 43.9 percent. The data do not support the simple idea that more maternal schooling always means lower violent discipline.

sections.heading: Violent discipline was highest in the fourth wealth quintile
sections.body: In 2025, any violent discipline was highest in the fourth wealth quintile, at 60.4 percent. The highest quintile had the lowest level, at 47.2 percent. The middle quintile was also high at 56.4 percent, followed by the second quintile at 55.4 percent and the lowest quintile at 53.4 percent. Psychological aggression and physical punishment followed a similar pattern, with the fourth quintile highest on both measures. Compared with 2022, the largest declines were in the highest, middle, and second quintiles, while the fourth quintile was nearly unchanged.

takeaways: Nationally, any violent discipline fell from 58.8 percent in 2022 to 54.7 percent in 2025, but it still affected more than half of children aged 1 to 14 years.
Regional differences were large in 2025, ranging from 32.7 percent in BARMM to 70.9 percent in Davao Region.
Most headline regional differences came from psychological aggression and any physical punishment, while severe physical punishment was much less common.
In 2025, exposure was higher among children aged 3 to 9, boys, urban children, and children in the fourth wealth quintile than among their comparison groups.
footnotes: ¹ Includes the province of Sulu in 2025.
